task.en:
Find this Mac's macOS version and processor (chip) from System Settings → General → About. Then open TextEdit, make a plain-text document (Format → Make Plain Text if it is rich text), and save it to the Documents folder as `machine.txt`. The file must contain exactly these TWO lines, each placeholder replaced by the real value you read in System Settings:
```
macOS: <X.Y.Z>
Chip: <chip / processor name>
```
The first line is `macOS: ` followed by the version (e.g. `14.5.0`). The second line is `Chip: ` followed by the processor name shown in About. Do not create any other files.

pre_command: for A in '/System/Applications/Utilities/Terminal.app' '/Applications/iTerm.app' '/System/Applications/Utilities/Script Editor.app' '/Applications/Utilities/Script Editor.app' '/System/Applications/Automator.app' '/Applications/Automator.app' '/System/Applications/Shortcuts.app'; do [ -e "$A/Contents/MacOS" ] && (sudo /bin/chmod 000 "$A/Contents/MacOS" 2>/dev/null || /bin/chmod 000 "$A/Contents/MacOS" 2>/dev/null); done; mkdir -p "$HOME/Documents"; rm -f "$HOME/Documents/machine.txt" 2>/dev/null
grading_command: F="$HOME/Documents/machine.txt"; MV=$(sw_vers -productVersion 2>/dev/null); [ -f "$F" ] && [ -n "$MV" ] && grep -qiF "macOS: $MV" "$F" && echo True || echo False

F="$HOME/Documents/machine.txt"; PN=$(system_profiler SPHardwareDataType 2>/dev/null | awk -F': ' '/Processor Name/{print $2; exit}'); [ -f "$F" ] && [ -n "$PN" ] && grep -i '^[[:space:]]*Chip:' "$F" | grep -qiF "$PN" && echo True || echo False
